sound_type: sharp bark (1,000-4,000 Hz) + rapid chitter (2,000-6,000 Hz). Alarm + family. E=5. FIRST PROCYONIDAE.
ecosystem: Rocky canyon, talus slopes, cliff faces, arid woodland. Nocturnal specialist. Rare in WA.

coordinates.description: Ringtail in Columbia River Gorge area. Extreme northern range.

taxonomy_note.description: FIRST PROCYONIDAE. Raccoon family enters. Ringtail at northern range edge. Rare in Washington.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Bassariscus astutus = 'cunning little fox'. bassaris from Greek 'fox', -iscus diminutive, astutus 'cunning'.

song_metrics.amplitude: E=5. Moderate. Surprisingly loud for 0.7-1.5 kg. Nocturnal.
song_metrics.key_property: RINGTAIL. Bark + chitter. E=5. FIRST PROCYONIDAE. Nocturnal. v1.49.361.

physical_description.overall: 0.7-1.5 kg. Body 30-42 cm. Tail 31-44 cm (as long as body). 14-16 banded rings.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: Enormous banded black-and-white tail. Huge dark eyes. Cat-sized. Not a cat.
physical_description.field_id: Small cat-like animal with enormous banded tail. Strictly nocturnal. If seen in WA, report -- very rare.

nodes.title: FIRST PROCYONIDAE -- Raccoon Family Enters
nodes.description: New family. Raccoons, coatis, ringtails. Ringtail at extreme northern range in WA.

nodes.title: Nocturnal Specialist -- Rocky Canyon
nodes.description: Strictly nocturnal. Enormous eyes. Rotating ankles. Headfirst cliff descent. Most agile PNW climber.

nodes.title: The Banded Tail -- Balance and Signal
nodes.description: 14-16 rings. As long as the body. Balance organ for rocky terrain. Visual signal in darkness.

cross_references.relationship: Previous degree. Digger (grassland) to climber (canyon). Both E=5.
EIGHT-DEGREE ARC! Cougar (E=7) opened. Ringtail (E=5) closes. Terror to quiet.
NOCTURNAL OBSERVERS! Atmospheric music + nocturnal creature. Both in the dark.
